turns.text: Hello Dulcie, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the middle of your pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s a strange thing to be on this side of the exam table. For so long, I was the one standing at the whiteboard, detailing the mechanics of the luteal phase or explaining the nuances of rare gestational complications to students. Now, I’m the one sitting in the paper gown, listening to the doppler, and honestly? It’s harder than I ever let on to my students.

I’m in my second trimester, and my husband, Mark, keeps telling me to just "breathe and be," but my brain is a runaway train of medical literature. Every time I feel a flutter, instead of just feeling that rush of maternal joy, I’m mentally cross-referencing it with obscure case studies I used to teach. I catch myself scanning the ultrasound screen, looking for things that aren’t there, waiting for the "rare phenomena" to manifest. It’s like a curse—knowing too much makes me feel like I’m constantly waiting for a shoe to drop, even though my midwife, who is lovely, reminds me at every visit that everything is perfectly boring and healthy. 

I want to stop analyzing my own womb like a research project, you know? I want to just be Dulcie, the mom of three, not the fertility educator who can’t turn her internal textbook off. It's a work in progress. Shall we start?

When you first found out you were pregnant, what did you expect your first antenatal appointments to be like — the environment, the people, the information you'd receive? And what were they actually like?

Honestly, when I saw that second line on the test, I think I went into "clinical mode" immediately. I expected to walk into the office and feel that same professional distance I’d spent my career cultivating. I prepared for the standard intake questions, the routine urine dip, the quick chat about folic acid—all the things I’d recited a thousand times to others. I thought I’d be the one in control, the patient who knew all the acronyms and exactly what to expect from every screening.

But the reality was much more vulnerable. When I actually walked in for that first visit in Mansfield, the environment felt… overwhelming. I expected to feel empowered by my knowledge, but I actually felt exposed. The midwife was kind—truly, she’s been wonderful—but I could see that flicker of hesitation when she scanned my chart and saw my background. I realized then that I wasn’t just a patient; I was a peer, and that changed the dynamic. 

Instead of a simple exchange of information, I felt like I was constantly self-editing, holding back questions about obscure placental issues just so I wouldn’t seem like a "difficult" patient. I wanted a soft landing, but instead, I felt like I was back in the classroom, only this time, the lesson was about my own body. It wasn't the detached, expert experience I anticipated; it was deeply, uncomfortably personal.
Before your screening tests and scans, what did you expect the process of understanding your results to be like — how information would be given, by whom, and in what way? And what was the reality?

I’ll be honest—I expected the "results" phase to be like a professional consultation, similar to how I’d walk a client through a lab report. I pictured myself having an objective, data-driven conversation. I thought I’d be able to look at the numbers, analyze the statistics, and compartmentalize the information as purely medical. I expected a crisp, efficient delivery of information that respected my baseline knowledge.

But when it came time for the actual scans, that clinical detachment completely evaporated. Standing in front of that monitor, I wasn't an educator; I was just a mother holding her breath. When the technician would pause—even for a split second to get a better angle—my brain wouldn't just see a scan. It would immediately jump to the worst-case, textbook-perfect scenario for some rare anomaly. 

The reality was that the information felt much more fragile than any data sheet I’d ever worked with. My midwife is wonderful, but sometimes she gives me this look—like she’s wondering if I’m waiting for her to say something specific, or if I’m going to challenge her interpretation. It makes me want to scream, *“I don’t want the expert view! I just want you to tell me my baby is okay.”* It’s a paradox: I have all the language to ask for details, but what I really crave is simple, human reassurance.
Was there a gap between the amount of information you were given and the amount you felt you needed to make sense of your results?

That is the million-dollar question, isn't it? And it’s such a complicated gap to bridge. 

The clinical answer is that my midwife provides the standard, evidence-based information that anyone else would get. But because of my background, there’s this internal conflict: part of me wants *everything*—every statistic, every possible outcome, the raw data behind the curves. I have this reflexive need to have the full picture because I spent my life believing that information is power, and power is control. 

However, the "Dulcie" who is currently growing a human being? She feels like she’s drowning in that same information. There’s a massive gap between the *data* I have access to and the *comfort* I actually need. When I get a result, I don’t just process it; I interrogate it. I turn it over and over, looking for hidden variables. So, even when my provider gives me the "all clear," I’m the one filling in the blanks with "what ifs" because I know too many of them. 

The irony isn't lost on me. I’m drowning in information that I’m perfectly capable of understanding, yet it does absolutely nothing to lower my cortisol levels. I don't need more charts; I need someone to tell me that the statistics don't apply to this specific, beautiful, squirming baby. That’s the information I’m actually looking for, and it’s the one thing that can't be printed on a lab report.
During your pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of having a consistent midwife or care provider who knew your history and situation? And what continuity of care did you actually experience?

I expected a sense of partnership—a continuity that felt like a quiet, steady rhythm. I wanted that "midwife-led" ideal where we’d move past the formalities of my chart and reach a point where she knew not just my risk factors, but *me*. I wanted a provider who recognized the woman behind the "Fertility Educator" tag in my file and could navigate the boundary between my clinical brain and my anxious maternal heart.

In reality, it’s been a bit of a tug-of-war. My midwife is lovely, truly, but there’s a persistent professional wall that feels reinforced by my own background. Because I know the system, I think she sometimes feels she doesn't need to "explain" things in the same way she would with a first-time mother. She assumes I already know what she’s going to say, which leaves me feeling a bit isolated, as if I’m expected to be my own clinician.

There isn't a lack of clinical continuity, but there is a lack of *emotional* continuity. I’m seeing the same face, yes, but I feel like I have to work twice as hard to signal that I want to be held, not consulted. It makes the office feel less like a sanctuary and more like a place where I’m constantly negotiating the terms of my own care.
What was the impact — practically or emotionally — of seeing different providers at different appointments?

Thankfully, I’ve managed to stay with my primary midwife for the bulk of my appointments, but there was one instance where I saw a covering provider, and it was jarring. Practically speaking, it’s a hurdle because you have to summarize your "story" so quickly. You start with the baseline—healthy, low-risk, no complications—but I found myself having to over-explain just to bridge the gap. It felt like I was back in a classroom, giving a condensed lecture on my own physiology to a stranger who didn't know how to navigate my specific brand of "informed" anxiety.

Emotionally, that disconnect hit me harder than I expected. When you’re already prone to scanning your own body for textbook anomalies, having a new face walk in means you don't have that established shorthand. There’s no trust built up yet, so I felt like I had to "perform" wellness. I caught myself downplaying my worries because I didn't want to come across as the "difficult, over-educated patient" to someone who didn’t know me. 

It made me realize how much of the comfort in maternity care relies on that hidden, unspoken understanding. When that’s missing, I feel the "clinical distance" widen, and that’s when I start feeling like a research subject again, rather than a woman who just wants to hear a heartbeat and go home.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of how your changing body would be discussed and examined by healthcare providers? And how did those interactions actually feel?

I think I expected it to be a very professional, almost objective assessment—like how I used to talk about "fundal height" or "symphysis-fundal height measurements" as if they were just variables in a textbook. I anticipated that my body would be treated as a biological vessel, and I honestly thought I’d be okay with that. I thought I could handle the clinical gaze because I’ve *been* the clinical gaze for years.

But when I’m actually on the table, it feels so much more exposed than the science ever suggested. When they pull the gown back or measure my abdomen, I don’t just feel the tape measure; I feel a strange sense of loss. I’m not just a set of clinical markers—like weight gain or blood pressure—I’m a woman who feels like her body is rapidly becoming a stranger’s territory. 

When a provider is strictly clinical, I feel that distance keenly. It’s like they’re checking the stats on a car rather than checking in with a mother. I find myself wishing, even for a moment, that they’d acknowledge the *experience* of the change, not just the data points. I want someone to notice that I’m tired, or that I’m nervous, rather than just marking "normal" in my chart. It’s a very lonely feeling to be medically "doing great" while feeling emotionally untethered.

Before pregnancy, what did you expect the experience of managing a high-risk condition like gestational diabetes or pre-eclampsia to involve? If relevant, how did the reality of managing it differ from your expectations?

It’s interesting you ask this, because although I’m technically "low-risk," my brain treats every day like I’m managing a high-risk diagnosis. 

In my professional life, I’ve taught the protocols for gestational diabetes and pre-eclampsia until I was blue in the face. I expected the management of those to be a rigorous, black-and-white process: monitor, intervene, stabilize. I used to think the patients I saw who were anxious were just struggling with the lifestyle changes or the fear of the unknown. I thought the *medicine* was the solution to the stress.

But the reality is that the "management" of a condition isn't just about blood glucose logs or blood pressure readings; it’s about the psychological weight of vigilance. Now that I’m the one sitting in the chair, I realize that even without a complication, the mental burden of "monitoring" is relentless. I look at my own body and I see a potential disaster area waiting to happen. 

If I *did* have a condition like pre-eclampsia, I imagine the reality would be an absolute nightmare of hyper-analysis. I’d be obsessing over every headache or bit of swelling, not because I don’t trust my providers, but because I know exactly how fast things can turn. The gap between expectation and reality is that the "protocol" doesn't touch the fear—it only fuels the data-gathering. Being "low risk" feels like walking on a tightrope, and I’m terrified that my knowledge is the wind pushing me off.
What was missing from the support offered — information, emotional acknowledgement, practical help?

What’s missing is the permission to be something other than an expert. 

In every appointment, there’s this unspoken assumption that because I know the "what" and the "how" of pregnancy, I don’t need the "why" or the reassurance. The support I’m offered is strictly clinical—my blood pressure is fine, the baby is growing, the markers are perfect. But there’s a total absence of emotional acknowledgement for the person who is simultaneously a mother and a former lecturer. 

I don't just need the facts about my placental health; I need someone to say, "Dulcie, I know you know all the risks, but your body is doing exactly what it's supposed to do." I need them to see that my "professionalism" is actually a thin, brittle shell for a lot of quiet, persistent anxiety. 

Practically, I think I could really use someone to talk to who understands that my knowledge is a double-edged sword. I’m not looking for a medical consultation; I’m looking for a human connection that acknowledges how hard it is to turn off the clinical part of my brain. I feel like I’m in a weird limbo where I’m too smart to be treated like a typical patient, but too scared to be treated like a colleague. I just want to be seen as a mom who needs a little grace, not just a clean bill of health.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of being able to access care promptly when you had a concern — whether urgent or not? And how responsive was the system when you needed it?

I expected the system to be a responsive, well-oiled machine. I’ve worked in clinical environments, so I have this internal map of triage, urgency, and the standard turnaround times for questions. I thought that if I had a genuine concern—even if it was just me spiraling—I’d have a direct line to someone who could de-escalate the situation, not just by giving me a textbook answer, but by hearing my concern as a parent.

The reality, however, is that "access" often feels like hitting a bureaucratic wall. When I’ve reached out—sometimes for things that were probably just my own over-analyzing—the response has been efficient but sterile. It’s almost always filtered through administrative hurdles: leaving a message for a nurse, waiting for a call back, or getting an answer through an online portal that is purely data-driven.

It’s efficient for medical triage, sure. But it’s not *responsive* to the human who’s currently feeling the panic. When I’m sitting at home in Mansfield, staring at my reflection in the window and feeling that familiar, creeping dread, a portal notification telling me my vitals are "within normal limits" feels cold. It leaves me feeling like I’ve bothered a system that doesn’t have room for my humanity, only my statistics. It’s isolating when the "prompt" care is technically there, but it lacks the warmth I actually need.
Were there times you delayed seeking help because of how difficult it was to access care, or because you worried about being judged for contacting services?

Absolutely. And that’s the most frustrating part of my experience, because I know better. I’ve spent my career telling women, "If you’re worried, just call. Better to be safe." But when it’s me? I absolutely hesitate.

It’s a mix of both things you mentioned. First, there’s the sheer exhaustion of having to go through the administrative gatekeepers—the automated phone systems, the triage nurse who doesn’t know me, the feeling of having to justify my concern before I’m even allowed to speak to my midwife. It’s a hurdle that makes you stop and ask, *“Is this worth the effort?”* 

But more than that, it’s the fear of the label. I am so terrified of being "that" patient—the one who knows just enough to be annoying, the one who calls because she’s over-analyzing a symptom that her training tells her is probably benign. I don’t want to be dismissed as an anxious professional who’s "googling" her own pregnancy, even though I’m not googling; I’m accessing my own internalized database. 

I’ve definitely sat on things for hours, sometimes days, just turning the symptoms over in my head, weighing the cost of the call versus the cost of my pride. It’s pathetic, really. I’m thirty, I’ve got two kids already, and I’m letting my professional ego get in the way of peace of mind. It’s the ultimate irony.
During pregnancy, what did you expect the role of your family, friends, or community to be in supporting you? And how did that informal support network actually show up — or not?

I expected my informal network to be a soft place to land. I thought my friends and family would be the ones to help me leave the "clinical" headspace behind, reminding me of the simple, joyful reality of bringing a new life into our home. I wanted them to be the ones who saw Dulcie, the mom of three, not the fertility expert who knows the exact percentage of risk for any given complication.

For the most part, my husband, Mark, has been my anchor. He’s the only one who truly gets it; he’s the one who hears me spiraling and gently reminds me that I don’t have to "teach" the hospital staff anything. He tries so hard to bring me back to the present.

But there’s a strange disconnect with the rest of my community. Because I spent years being the "expert" for everyone else, my friends often treat me like a repository of information. They ask *me* about their pregnancy symptoms or complications, and I find myself slipping back into teacher mode automatically. It’s hard to tell them, "I don't have the bandwidth for this," because I’ve spent my career making them feel safe. I expected my community to support me, but I often feel like I’m still the one supporting them. It makes me feel incredibly isolated, like I’m the only one allowed to have a "textbook" pregnancy, which is the last thing I want.
Were there forms of support you received from family or community that you hadn't expected — or support you expected that didn't materialise?

I suppose I didn't expect the weight of being the "oracle" in my friend group to feel so heavy during my own pregnancy. I expected my friends to show up and just hold space for *me*, but instead, I’m the one fielding questions about hormone levels and early-pregnancy spotting. It’s an exhausting, unexpected labor. They seem to assume that because I know the clinical pathways, I’m immune to the fear that comes with them—as if my knowledge somehow acts as a protective shield against the uncertainty of birth. It doesn't. It just gives me a clearer picture of what could possibly go wrong.

What I expected, and what simply didn't materialize, was the opportunity to just be a "regular" mom. I wanted someone to stop asking for my expert opinion and start asking how *I* was feeling—the emotional, messy, un-clinical *me*. 

On the flip side, I didn’t expect how vital Mark’s quiet, persistent presence would be. He’s become this expert at reading my face. He doesn’t try to solve my problems with statistics, which is what I’m always tempted to do. He’ll just put a hand on my back or change the subject entirely to something mundane, like the nursery paint or what the older kids are doing. That kind of non-clinical support? It’s the only thing that actually grounds me.

I’ve been thinking more about that balance—or lack of it. It’s hard to articulate, but there’s this strange performative exhaustion in my appointments. I feel like I’m constantly "managing" the provider’s perception of me. If I don't ask the "right" questions, they might think I’m checking out or being blasé, but if I ask the *deep* questions—the ones that keep me up at night—I fear being labeled the "difficult, over-informed" patient who is, quite frankly, a nuisance. 

There’s a specific moment I keep replaying in my mind: the last scan. I caught myself explaining the technician’s screen back to her, almost like I was lecturing. I heard my own voice, and I just wanted to stop—I wanted to clamp my hand over my mouth and just say, "Please, just tell me if my baby is okay." But I couldn't. The habit of being the educator is so deeply ingrained in my speech patterns that it feels like a reflex I can’t control. 

I’m worried that as I get closer to the third trimester, this will only intensify. I just want to find a way to let go of the "expert" persona before the delivery room. I want my medical team to see me as a mother who is simply, profoundly human—not as a peer who needs to be handled with professional kid gloves. Is that too much to ask for? To just be allowed to be vulnerable without it being analyzed?
Were there moments when a doctor, partner, or family member's opinion overrode yours — even when you hadn't asked for their input?

Oh, absolutely. It’s almost constant, honestly. Because of my background, people assume that my "informed" perspective is just another data point to be debated rather than a lived experience to be respected. 

There was a moment recently with my midwife where I raised a concern about a specific sensation—something I’d felt a hundred times in my professional life—and she immediately pivoted to the standard, low-risk, "textbook" explanation. She gave me that tight, patient smile that says, *“Oh, you’re just reading too much into it, Dulcie.”* It felt like she was overriding my intuition because she decided I was just being the "over-analyzing educator" again. I didn't push back—I couldn't—because I felt so patronized that I just shut down. 

Then there’s the family dynamic. My mother, bless her, thinks my clinical background is a magical barrier against worry. If I express a fear, she’ll say, "Well, you’re the expert, Dulcie, you know the stats, you know it’s fine." It effectively silences me. She’s using my own knowledge against me to shut down my emotional needs. It’s like my intellect has become a cage; because I’m "supposed" to know better, I’m not allowed to just feel the ordinary, messy fear that any other pregnant woman would be allowed to express. My opinions, my worries, and my intuition get dismissed as just… "professional noise." It’s incredibly isolating to be told you're "too smart" to be worried, when you're actually just a mom who is terrified of the unknown.

I think what’s starting to weigh on me most is the looming shadow of the delivery room. I’ve read all the obstetric journals. I’ve seen the charts on outcomes for every single scenario. I have a very vivid, high-definition mental image of every possible deviation from a "normal" birth, and I know exactly what interventions follow each one. 

When I look at my midwife now, I don't see someone who’s there to facilitate my transition into motherhood. I see a practitioner whose standard protocols for labor management are going to clash with the specific, nuanced birth plan I’ve built in my head. I’m already bracing for that power struggle. I can hear myself starting to lecture, trying to steer the ship because I’m terrified that if I relinquish control—if I let myself be just a patient—I’ll lose my agency entirely. 

But I’m exhausted by it. I don't want to be the "informed patient" who manages her own labor. I want to be held. I want to feel safe enough to let my guard down, to stop being the one who anticipates every physiological step and just let my body do what it’s built to do. But I feel trapped. Every time I try to signal that I need support, I fall back into the language of medicine, and the cycle repeats. It’s like being locked in a room where the only way to get out is to speak a language I’m desperate to stop using. I just want to stop being a textbook and finally be a mother.
Were there specific decisions — such as timing, fertility testing, or lifestyle changes — where you felt pressure to align with your partner's preferences over your own?

That is a really complicated question. Mark is an incredible husband, but yes, there’s been a subtle, pervasive pressure to align with his "keep calm and carry on" approach. Because he isn't in the clinical world, his way of managing stress is to push the data away. He sees my research and my constant monitoring as *causes* of my anxiety, not responses to it.

When it came to, say, our choices about prenatal screening, I felt this intense pressure to lean toward his perspective of "what we don't know won't hurt us." He wanted to wait and see, to keep things simple, while I was already looking at the risk-benefit analysis for every possible test. I felt like if I pushed for the screenings I thought were important, I was being the "difficult, anxious expert" and forcing him into a space of worry he didn't want to inhabit. 

So, I often found myself choosing his "laid-back" route just to keep the peace. I didn't want to be the one to introduce a "what-if" into our home. It’s hard to advocate for your own intuition when your partner views that intuition as a professional neurosis. I often feel like I’m compromising my own sense of preparedness just to protect our domestic bubble from the clinical reality I live in every day at work. It feels like I have to hide my brain to keep our relationship feeling "normal," which just makes me feel more disconnected from my own pregnancy.

I think the hardest part is that I’m mourning the pregnancy I *thought* I would have. I truly believed that because I knew all the mechanics—the hormonal surges, the placental function, the fetal development—I’d be the gold standard of patients. I thought I’d be the one who wasn't afraid of the process because the process was my life’s work. 

Now, I look at the other women in the waiting room, the ones who seem to have no idea what’s happening on a cellular level, and I feel a pang of envy. They have this beautiful, blissful ignorance. They don't know the exact failure rate of a specific diagnostic test; they just know that the midwife said "everything looks great," and they trust it. They walk out of the clinic with a smile. I walk out with a mental checklist of every single thing that could still go wrong in the next twenty-four hours.

My professional life has given me all the data but none of the peace. I keep trying to explain to Mark that I don’t *want* to be this way, that I’d give anything to just be a passenger in my own body, but I don’t even know how to shut that part of my brain off anymore. It feels like I’ve been living in a high-stakes exam room for ten years, and now that I’m the one being tested, I can't stop grading the invigilator. I’m just so tired of being the expert on my own fear. I just want someone to take the clipboard away.
Were you given unsolicited comments about your weight, diet, or physical appearance? How did those comments land?

It’s funny, because my background usually protects me from the "you’re eating for two" kind of myths, but it invites a different, colder kind of commentary. Because my providers—and even my friends—see me as the "clinically literate" one, they treat my body like a set of variables to be optimized rather than a person.

I’ve had providers point at my weight gain or my fluid intake with this jarring, detached efficiency. It’s not "Oh, you look radiant," it’s "Your BMI is tracking toward the upper quartile for your gestation, we should watch that." It feels like I’m being graded. I know the clinical rationale behind monitoring weight—I’ve taught it!—but hearing it applied to my own body, stripped of any warmth, feels like a violation. It makes me feel less like a mother-to-be and more like a specimen being assessed for protocol adherence.

And my friends? They’re worse in a different way. They assume because I’m an "expert," I’m immune to body image issues. They’ll say things like, "Oh, you already know the science of why you’re swelling, so it doesn't bother you, right?" It makes me feel like I have to perform a certain level of stoicism. I can’t admit that I’m struggling with the loss of my pre-pregnancy body or the discomfort of the extra weight, because that would break the character of the "all-knowing professional" they’ve projected onto me. I feel like I’m constantly walking through a hall of mirrors where no one sees me, just the degree on my wall.

I keep coming back to the idea that I’m living in a professional echo chamber. Everywhere I go—the office, the school where I taught, even my home—my "expert" identity precedes me. It’s a bit like being a ghost in your own life. People look at my bump and they don't see Dulcie, the mom who’s terrified of a minor complication; they see a walking, talking medical textbook.

I think the biggest toll this has taken is that I’ve stopped being able to advocate for my own simple, human needs because I’m so focused on navigating the *professional* expectations of everyone around me. When I’m at the clinic, I’m "the colleague," not "the mother." When I’m at home, I’m "the teacher," not "the partner." I feel like I have to keep the mask on for everyone. I don’t think I’ve truly let myself be *just* the mother of this child, not even for a minute, because I feel like I have to keep up this standard of being informed, prepared, and composed. 

Sometimes, I find myself standing in front of the mirror, just looking at my belly and wishing I could strip away all the clinical vocabulary I’ve accumulated. I want to see a miracle, not a case study. I want to see a future, not a list of risks. I want, more than anything, for someone to finally just look at me and say, "Dulcie, you don't have to know everything. It's okay to not be the expert for once." But I’m afraid if I say that out loud, the illusion will shatter, and I’ll be left with nothing but my own vulnerability. And honestly? That's a terrifying place to be.

It’s funny, the more I speak about this, the more I realize that the "curse" I’ve been complaining about is really a shield. I spent my whole career teaching women that knowledge is the ultimate antidote to fear—that if you understand the physiology, you can master the experience. But in my own case, it’s backfired. I’m a student of my own biology, and I’m failing the class because I’m trying to be the teacher and the pupil simultaneously.

I wonder if I’ll ever be able to walk into the delivery room and just surrender to the process. Right now, I can see the timeline of my own labor so clearly—the contractions, the dilation, the potential for stalling—that I feel like I’m already there, exhausted and over-analyzing the monitor. I’m terrified that when the time comes, I’ll be so busy managing the clinical side of my birth that I’ll miss the moment of meeting my baby. I’ll be checking my own vitals instead of looking into their eyes.

I think the biggest regret I have—if I can call it that—is that I haven’t given myself permission to be a "patient" in the truest sense of the word. A patient is someone who suffers, someone who waits, someone who is cared for. I’ve been so busy being the "professional" that I’ve denied myself the grace of being cared for. Maybe the next time I go to Mansfield for an appointment, I’ll try to leave the educator at the door. Maybe I’ll walk in, sit on that paper, and for once, tell my midwife, "I don't know what’s happening, and I’m scared. Can you just tell me a story where everything is fine?" It sounds so simple, but for someone like me, it feels like the hardest thing in the world to ask.
Were there appointments or decisions where you felt your input was genuinely sought, versus times when you felt you were simply being informed of a plan?

It’s a subtle but painful distinction. When I’m asked about my preferences for something like routine screening—which I’ve explained to hundreds of students—it feels like a test, not a partnership. The midwife will look at me, notebook poised, and ask what *I* think we should do. In those moments, I feel the weight of my resume hanging in the air. I’m not being asked for my intuition as a mother; I’m being asked to validate the clinical algorithm. It feels like she’s asking me to peer-review her practice, which is the last thing I want to do.

Conversely, there have been moments—like when we discussed the logistics of my maternity leave or the simple, non-clinical aspects of the birth plan—where the conversation shifts. In those brief windows, I’m not the "Expert Educator," I’m just Dulcie. Those are the only times I feel like my input is actually sought. It’s when the discussion moves away from the biological "how" and into the human "what would make this easier for you?" 

But the vast majority of the time? I’m being informed of the plan. I’m told what the schedule is, what the tests are, and what the protocol dictates. I’m treated like a participant in a predefined workflow rather than a collaborator in my own care. They explain the "why" with such clinical certainty that there’s no room for me to ask, "But what if I want to do it differently?" I’ve become so accustomed to the script that I’ve stopped trying to improvise. I just nod, sign the form, and try to suppress the part of me that wants to ask for a different approach. It’s a very lonely, pre-scripted way to grow a baby.

It’s funny, the further along I get, the more I realize that the "professional mask" is becoming heavier, like armor that’s starting to chafe. I keep waiting for the point where I can just walk into that clinic and be… empty. By that, I mean empty of the textbooks, the case studies, the lecture notes, and the constant, buzzing background noise of medical data. I want to arrive at my next appointment with a clean slate, but I don’t think that’s physically possible anymore.

I find myself envying the women who can look at the ultrasound machine and just see their baby’s heartbeat as a miracle. I look at it and I see the *trimester*, the *gestational age*, the *cardiac rhythm*, the *possibilities* and the *risk markers*. It’s a tragedy, really, to have all this knowledge and yet feel so profoundly disconnected from the wonder of it all. I feel like I’ve been analyzing the paint on the walls so intensely that I’ve missed the fact that I’m standing in a cathedral. 

I’m hopeful, though—or maybe I’m just desperate—that when the actual labor starts, the intensity will finally drown out the professional voice in my head. I’m hoping that the sheer, raw, physical reality of birth will be so overwhelming that it forces the "Educator" to sit down and be quiet. Maybe that’s the only way to get my agency back. I’m just trying to keep my head above water until then, hoping that someone—my midwife, Mark, or even just my own baby—will see past the clinical profile and just hold my hand. I don’t want to be a colleague anymore. I just want to be a mother.
Was there a moment when your trust in a provider was broken — something they said, missed, or failed to explain?

It happened during a routine scan early on. I had mentioned a minor, specific ache—something common, but in the context of my knowledge, I was just checking to see if they’d flag anything related to placental positioning. The midwife gave me that familiar, practiced smile and breezed over it with a, "Oh, Dulcie, you know as well as I do that’s just round ligament pain."

In that second, something inside me just… snapped. She wasn't wrong, but she had used my own expertise as a weapon to dismiss my intuition. She didn't look at my *concern*; she looked at my *resume*. It wasn’t a medical error—it was a failure of care. I realized then that she wasn't seeing me at all; she was just seeing a peer she didn't have to worry about comforting.

It broke a little piece of the trust I’d tried so hard to build. I realized that my own knowledge had effectively "opted me out" of the bedside manner I desperately needed. I couldn't be a patient because I was a professional, and I couldn't be a professional because I was a patient. I felt profoundly invisible. It’s a bitter pill to realize that being "the expert" is just another way to be alienated. I don't go to appointments with the same openness now; I go with my guard up, keeping my mouth shut, just waiting for the next time I’m told what I already know.

The loneliness of it is really what stays with you. I find myself sitting in my car after an appointment, staring at the rearview mirror and just breathing, waiting for my heart rate to normalize. It’s like I’ve been running a marathon, but all I did was sit in a chair and answer questions about my own physiology. 

I’m starting to realize that the disconnect isn't just with my midwife or the office staff—it’s with the *entire system*. We’ve built this culture where "informed consent" and "evidence-based care" are the highest virtues, and don’t get me wrong, they are. But we’ve forgotten that those aren't enough to carry someone through the sheer existential weight of pregnancy. You can be the most informed person in the room and still be terrified, still be grieving your old life, still be wondering if you’re actually going to survive the transition into motherhood. 

When I look back at my students—the young couples I used to teach—I feel this wave of guilt. I gave them the biology, the risks, the interventions, and the mechanics. I thought I was giving them agency, but now I see I was just giving them the vocabulary for their own fear. I never told them that the most important thing you need in that room isn't just the knowledge—it’s the person who looks you in the eye and tells you that it’s okay to be small, to be scared, and to not know what’s coming next. I wish I could go back and tell them, and tell myself, that it’s alright to put the textbook away. I’m just trying to learn that lesson now, as slowly and painfully as I am.
Were there appointments, scans, or care moments where your partner's absence left you feeling isolated or unsupported?

That’s a heavy question. Mark has been amazing about trying to make it to every scan, but there have been those few, unavoidable times when his work kept him away. Those are the appointments that feel like a cold splash of water.

When Mark is there, he’s this grounding wire. He doesn’t have the medical vocabulary, so he doesn’t try to participate in the "expert" dialogue. He’s just there as my partner, which forces me to step out of the lecturer persona. Without him, there’s no one to remind me that I’m allowed to stop being the one who knows everything. I find myself standing there with the provider, and because there’s no "us" to buffer the conversation, the dynamic immediately slips back into that sterile, colleague-to-colleague exchange.

It’s isolating because I realize that the *reason* I feel so alone in this isn't just the system—it’s that I don’t have a witness to my vulnerability. When Mark is in the room, I can’t hide behind my professional knowledge as effectively; he’ll call me out if I’m getting too clinical or if he sees that I’m drifting into that anxious headspace. When he’s not there, I’m left alone with my own thoughts and the medical, objective reality of the monitor. I become the "Fertility Educator" again by default. 

His absence makes me realize that I’ve been using him as a bridge to stay human. Without him there to anchor me, I just float away into a sea of "what-ifs" and case studies. It makes me wonder how any of this would feel if I didn't have him—or if I had to do this entirely on my own. It’s a frightening thought, and it makes me cherish his hand in mine during those scans more than I ever thought I would.

I keep wondering what I’d say if I could strip away the professional title on my chart. If I walked into my next appointment, looked my midwife in the eye, and confessed, "I know exactly what the literature says about my risk factors, but I am still terrified I’m going to lose this baby to something I’ve taught a hundred times," would she finally see me? Or would she just offer me a brochure on prenatal anxiety and move on to the next patient?

The irony is that I've built this entire career around helping others feel empowered through knowledge, yet I feel completely disempowered by my own. It’s like I’ve been handed a map of a dark forest, and because I can see every potential trap and pitfall on the paper, I’m paralyzed at the entrance. I’ve forgotten that the forest is actually just a path that thousands of people walk every day. 

I’m so tired of being the person who has to have the answers. I want to be the one who asks the questions, even the ones that sound "uninformed" or "naive." I want to be allowed to have a bad day without it being diagnosed. I think that's the final piece of the puzzle: I don't just want care; I want to be *seen* as someone who is allowed to not know. It’s the ultimate vulnerability for a woman who has spent her life being the teacher. I’m hoping, with everything I have, that I can find that version of myself before I step into that labor room. I need to be a mother first, and an expert second. Or maybe, just maybe, I can stop being an expert altogether.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think there is one thing, and it’s the strangest, most quiet irony of all. In my field, we talk about "informed choice" as if it’s the pinnacle of patient autonomy. We want patients to have every bit of data so they can make the "best" decision for their birth, for their baby, for their body. But there’s a cost to that level of information that we never discuss in the journals. 

When you know the mechanics of every single risk factor, you don’t feel like you’re making "choices" anymore. You feel like you’re constantly trying to outsmart a biology that doesn’t care about your expertise. Every decision—from my diet to my sleep schedule to the way I prepare for labor—feels like a desperate bid to win a game against nature that I know, statistically, I shouldn't even be worried about. 

I just want to add that for those of us who live inside the medical system, the biggest missing piece of care isn't better information—it’s the permission to be ignorant. It’s the grace to walk into a room and be allowed to say, "I have no idea what’s happening, and I trust you to hold the map for me." That’s what I’m craving. I want to be able to trust someone else to be the expert so that I can finally, for the first time in years, just be the mother. It’s the one thing they don't teach you in the textbooks, and it’s the one thing I’m finding I need the most. Thank you for listening; it felt... good, to finally say that out loud.
